chunk_summary: Clinical evaluation of adolescent thinness involves identifying causes like chronic undernutrition, systemic disease, or disordered eating, and implementing a structured follow-up plan addressing medical and nutritional needs.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical red flags for adolescent thinness, including weight centile crossing and delayed pubertal progression.
Learn how to evaluate meal regularity, micronutrient deficiencies, and systemic contributors to undernutrition.
Develop a follow-up plan combining medical treatment and structured nutritional interventions.
key_facts: Red flags include crossing down of weight centiles, slowed height velocity, and delayed pubertal progression.
Indian data link adolescent undernutrition to fatigue, poor school performance, and higher-risk pregnancy outcomes.
Screen for anemia with CBC when fatigue or pallor is present; consider iron, vitamin B12, and folate deficiencies.
Management includes daily protein and calcium sources, energy-dense nutrient-dense foods, and avoiding ultra-processed snacks.
Reassess weight trajectory and functional outcomes in 4–6 weeks; lack of catch-up growth warrants further evaluation.
reference_quote: Indian data emphasize functional and intergenerational consequences: adolescent undernutrition predicts fatigue, poor school performance, and higher-risk pregnancy outcomes when undernourished girls enter pregnancy.
document_key_topics: Red flags and systemic contributors to undernutrition
